titleStmt.title: Procedures for proper validation of record critical current density claims

analytic.title: Procedures for proper validation of record critical current density claims

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: High critical current density 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 is generally the most important property of any superconductor used for applications. The critical current 𝐼𝐼 𝑐𝑐 is most frequently measured by transport 4 probe V-I techniques and the measured 𝐼𝐼 𝑐𝑐 is then divided by the cross-sectional area to give the critical current density 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 . This method is normally error-free. Occasionally however, 𝐼𝐼 𝑐𝑐 and 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 are computed from measurements of the magnetic moment and two recent papers (Goyal et al. 2024 Nat. Commun. 15, 6523 and Polichetti et al. 2024 arXiv:2410.09197) illustrate some of the perils of reporting properties of REBa2Cu3O7-δ (REBCO) films grown on a coated conductor substrate using only magnetic evaluations. Correct data analysis is made difficult by the persistent use of cgs units in commercial instrument outputs and by misapplication of handbook Bean model formulae, which in this case led to an order of magnitude 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 overestimate, which of course renders the original world record claim invalid. The laudable attempt of the authors to explain their original mistake has unfortunately its own errors. Here we explain explicitly how errors are still present in the second paper and provide correct expressions for the conversion of magnetic moments to 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values using Bean model expressions. An important conclusion is that record 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values ought always to be validated by real transport measurements and by cross-reference to similar samples and their reported 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Recently an article by Goyal et al. [1] has attracted much attention because of its claim of world record critical current density values in REBa2Cu3O7-δ (REBCO, RE being rare earths) film samples. However, the values were obtained only by magnetic moment measurements, from which 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 was calculated using unstated expressions. In oral presentations of the results, it was several times suggested that the values were so high relative to the depairing current density that transport measurement verification was advisable but when the paper was published on August 7 th , 2024, only magnetic characterizations supported their claim of world-record 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values. Talantsev and Tallon [2] asked the authors for the raw data and recalculated 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 using SI formula and found that the actual 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 values were 10 times smaller than those reported [1] because of a unit conversion error. Once the error was revealed, the authors withdrew the paper (officially retracted on October 23 rd , 2024) [3]. In a follow up report, Polichetti et al. [4] explained how the error occurred and provided their own formulae as a guide to others of how to avoid similar error in the future.
In their follow up paper, Polichetti et al. [4] suggest the following expressions for correct 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 calculation:
div.formula: "To summarize: 2×𝛥𝛥𝛥𝛥[𝐴𝐴/𝑚𝑚] 𝑤𝑤[𝑚𝑚]×�1- 𝑤𝑤[𝑚𝑚] 3𝑏𝑏[𝑚𝑚] � = 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 [𝐴𝐴/𝑚𝑚 2 ] (3) or 2×𝛥𝛥𝛥𝛥�𝑒𝑒𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑒/𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚 3 � 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚]×�1- 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚] 3𝑏𝑏[𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚] � = 20×𝛥𝛥𝛥𝛥[𝐴𝐴/𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚] 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚]×�1- 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚] 3𝑏𝑏[𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚] � = 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 [𝐴𝐴/𝑐𝑐𝑚𝑚 2 ]
div.p: (4)" from ref. [4] However, equations 3 and 4 are inconsistent. Whereas equation (3) in ref. [4] is correct and in SI units, eq. ( 4) is incorrect in both the first and the second terms, as we show below. Indeed, as noted by Polichetti [4], a problem is that literature formulae are frequently expressed in different unit systems, not always specified, making unit conversion uncertain.

Because of our own experience in the difficulties of changing units and to avoid further proliferation of incorrect formulae, we decided to test the agreement of Polichetti's eqs. ( 3) and ( 4) , working from first principles. First, we calculate 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 in SI units for a sample of rectangular cross-section in perpendicular field using the simplest Bean model [5], [6] scenario, being explicit about how to properly convert the quantities into SI units. We then compare this formula against eqs. ( 3) and ( 4) in [4]. Then, since many still use cgs-emu units because of the way that the most popular magnetometers present their output, we present a converted formula allowing direct use of cgsemu quantities to 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 . This then reveals the errors in Polichetti et al.'s eq. ( 4) [4].

An important point of ref. [4] is to explain how easy it is to make such errors and it is thus absolutely pertinent to cite the final paragraph of Goldfarb's paper [7], which earlier explored this territory too. He cites and comments on a more than 100 year old paper by Giorgi [8]: "In introducing his rationalized, four-dimensional system, Giorgi [1901] wrote, Il sistema CGS, con questo, perde ogni ragione di esistere; ma non credo che il suo abbandono sarà lamentato da alcuno. ("With this, the CGS system loses every reason to exist; but I do not think that its abandonment will be lamented by anyone."). He may be correct, eventually." [7].

div.head: Bean model calculation of 𝑱𝑱 𝒄𝒄,𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎 in SI units and practical conversion
div.p: In SI units the moment generated by a current circulating in a loop is given by 𝑚𝑚 = 𝐼𝐼𝐴𝐴 𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙 , where 𝐼𝐼 is the current in [A] and 𝐴𝐴 𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙 is the area of the loop in [m 2 ] (notice that this equation is not valid in Gaussian units where 𝑚𝑚 = 𝐼𝐼𝐴𝐴 𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙 /𝑐𝑐; in fact Gaussian and cgs-emu units are the same only for magnetic properties as stated clearly in [9]). Assuming the simplest Bean scenario in which the field B has fully penetrated the film, the induced current in a sample of rectangular cross-section circulates in rectangular loops with the same absolute value of 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 at all points in the crosssection, as shown in Figure 1, and with no changes through the sample thickness 𝑡𝑡. Here we assume that the sample cross-section is 𝑤𝑤 × 𝑙𝑙 = 2𝑎𝑎 × 2𝑏𝑏, with 𝑎𝑎 < 𝑏𝑏. Considering the sketch in Figure 1 and its symmetry, the differential magnetic moment can be expressed as: The total moment 𝑚𝑚 generated by the supercurrent can be calculated by integration:
div.formula: 𝑑𝑑𝑚𝑚 = 𝑑𝑑𝐼𝐼𝐴𝐴 𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙 = (𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 𝑡𝑡𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡)(4(𝑏𝑏 -𝑎𝑎 + 𝑡𝑡)𝑡𝑡)
𝑚𝑚 = 4𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 𝑡𝑡 � (𝑏𝑏 -𝑎𝑎 + 𝑡𝑡)𝑡𝑡 𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡 𝑚𝑚 0 = 4𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 𝑡𝑡 � 𝑎𝑎 3 3 + 𝑎𝑎 2 2 (𝑏𝑏 -𝑎𝑎)�
div.p: Since the sample volume is 𝑉𝑉 = 4𝑎𝑎𝑏𝑏𝑡𝑡, and the magnetization is by definition 𝑀𝑀 = 𝑚𝑚 𝑉𝑉 ⁄ , we obtain:
The amplitude of the hysteresis loop is Δ𝑀𝑀 = 2𝑀𝑀, so:

Substituting 𝑎𝑎 = 𝑤𝑤/2 and 𝑏𝑏 = 𝑙𝑙/2, we obtain the SI formula for 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 : We notice that the SI formula in eq.( 1) is equivalent to the SI formula given by Talantsev and Tallon [2] and also matches Polichetti et al.'s eq.( 3) in [4], confirming its validity. As reported by Talantsev and Tallon, an equivalent practical and frequently used formula using quantities in cgs-emu units on its right side is: emu is a conversion factor with dimensions, not just a pure number. Notice that eq.( 2) is a practical converted formula that uses cgs-emu quantities as input data and an SI submultiple (A/cm 2 ) as output, so it is neither a cgs-emu nor an SI formula: in fact, Ampere is not the unit of current in cgs-emu, but abA (abampere) or Bi (biot) (in cgs-esu and Gaussian units, the current is expressed in statA or esu/s). This hybrid unit formula has clear potential dangers.
div.formula: 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 [ A m 2 ] = 2Δ𝛥𝛥[ A m ] 𝑤𝑤[m]�1- 𝑤𝑤[m] 3𝑙𝑙[m] � = 2Δ𝑚𝑚[Am 2 ] 𝑉𝑉[m 3 ]𝑤𝑤[m]�1- 𝑤𝑤[m] 3𝑙𝑙[m] � (SI units) (1
With 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 in [A/m 2 ], Δ𝑀𝑀 in [A/m],
𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 [ A cm 2 ] = 20 Acm 2 emu � Δ𝛥𝛥[ emu cm 3 ] 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m]�1- 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m] 3𝑙𝑙[𝑐𝑐m] � � = 20

div.head: Comparison with Polichetti's formulae
div.p: As verified above, Polichetti et al.'s eq.( 3) in [4] correctly corresponds to SI eq.( 1) here, but we also need to verify whether Polichetti et al.'s eq.( 4) provides the correct 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 value. It must be noted that these expressions are not proper cgs-emu formulae (A, Ampere, is not a cgs-emu unit). We verify the equations in Table 1 by comparing the results of the calculation with the different formulae. In the table ∆𝑚𝑚, 𝑤𝑤, 𝑙𝑙 and 𝑡𝑡, V, ∆M are all quantities in SI units, as shown in the 2 nd column. We use the already verified SI formula eq. ( 1) in the 2 nd column as a reference to evaluate the other proposed equations. The quantities in the following columns indicate the conversion of ∆𝑚𝑚, 𝑤𝑤, 𝑙𝑙 and 𝑡𝑡, 𝑉𝑉, ∆𝑀𝑀 into the appropriate units requested in the formulae in the 1 st row (e.g. in the 3 rd column ∆𝑚𝑚[emu] = (10 3 emu/(Am 2 ))∆𝑚𝑚[Am 2 ]), and we compare the results of the 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 calculation in the last row. From these calculations it is obvious that the formulae in the 2 nd and 3 rd columns, calculated from eqs. ( 1) and (2) of this paper, are correct with the expected factor of 10 -4 comparing 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 in A/m 2 and in A/cm 2 . However, columns 4 and 5 in Table 1, which use the 2 forms of Polichetti et al. eq.( 4), are clearly both incorrect, one being 10 times too small and the other 10 times too big because the pre-factors are incorrect.

div.head: Conclusions
div.p: The best approach to calculate 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 from magnetic measurements is by using SI units and formulae throughout, making the cgs-emu to SI conversion of magnetic moment 𝑚𝑚 right at the start. The present case shows that if a converted equation is desirable, its validity must be verified against a known valid equation to avoid the conversion error seen in ref. [4]. It is indeed probably true as suggested by Polichetti et al. that many errors have been made and not perhaps noticed. But in this case the error was important because a world record 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 was claimed.

In general, results reporting exceptional 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values should be independently verified and assessed, preferably by transport measurements. If, as in [1], only magnetic moment measurements were made, it is also highly advisable to provide the raw data, equation used to convert 𝑚𝑚 to 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 and all necessary information (like sample sizes for 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 calculation) needed for a proper data analysis to allow others to verify the results. This is how Talantsev and Tallon were able to find the error in the published 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 result. Comparison to similar samples could also have provided a useful skepticism in this case because films using BaZrO3 nanorod-containing vortex pinning centers were commercialized more than a decade ago by SuperPower and many reports have been published (e.g. [10], [11]). As an essential task of evaluating the many km of tape delivered for the 32 T user magnet at the NHMFL, it was observed that there was considerable variability of performance in such films. Francis et al. [12] evaluated the 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 spread of a variety of samples and found that the volume%, diameter and spacing of the BaZrO3 nanorods Table 1 Here the quantities ∆𝑚𝑚, 𝑤𝑤, 𝑙𝑙, 𝑡𝑡, 𝑉𝑉, ∆𝑀𝑀 are all in SI units as showed in the 2 nd column. In the following columns it is shown the conversion to obtain the quantities desired in the equations in the 1 st row. E.g. in the 3 rd column the value of ∆𝑚𝑚[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ] in SI units has to be multiplied by 10 3 𝑒𝑒𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑒/(𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ) to obtain the value in emu needed in the 1 st raw formula; so ∆𝑚𝑚[𝑒𝑒𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑒] = (10 3 𝑒𝑒𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑒/(𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ))∆𝑚𝑚 [𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ]. Note that the units of the pre-factor 2 are not provided in ref. [4] for eq.( 4) (1 st form); to make the units match, we assume the pre-factor 2 is actually 2 Acm 2 /emu (uncertainty marked by (?)). Correct SI formula, eq.( 1):
div.formula: 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 � A m 2 � = 2Δ𝑚𝑚[Am 2 ] 𝑉𝑉[m 3 ]𝑤𝑤[m] �1 - 𝑤𝑤[m] 3𝑙𝑙[m] �
div.p: Converted hybrid cgs/SI formula, eq.( 2): [4], eq.4, 1 st form:
div.formula: 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 � A cm 2 � = 20 Acm 2 emu � Δ𝑚𝑚[emu] 𝑉𝑉[cm 3 ]𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m] �1 - 𝑤𝑤[cm] 3𝑙𝑙[cm] � � Incorrect Polichetti et al.'s
2 × Δ𝑀𝑀 � emu cm 3 � 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m] × �1 - 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m] 3𝑏𝑏[𝑐𝑐m] � = 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 [𝐴𝐴/cm 2 ]
div.p: Incorrect Polichetti et al.'s [4], eq.4, 2 nd form: Finally, as advised more than a century ago by Giorgi [8] and pointed out by Goldfarb [7] in more recent years, it is probably time to abandon Gaussian or cgs-emu units for the preferable and better understood SI units and their immediate multiples and submultiples. We need to advocate now with the commercial magnetometer manufacturers!
div.formula: 20 × Δ𝑀𝑀 � 𝐴𝐴 cm � 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m] × �1 - 𝑤𝑤[𝑐𝑐m] 3𝑏𝑏[𝑐𝑐m] � = 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 [𝐴𝐴/cm 2 ] Δmoment Δ𝑚𝑚[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ] (10 3 𝑒𝑒𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑒/(𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ))∆𝑚𝑚[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ] Small side 𝑤𝑤[m] (10 2 cm/m)𝑤𝑤[m] (10
2 cm/m)𝑤𝑤[m] Large side 𝑙𝑙[m] (=𝑏𝑏 in eq.4 of [3]) (10 2 cm/m)𝑙𝑙[m] (10

2 cm/m)𝑙𝑙[m] (𝑙𝑙 = 𝑏𝑏 in eq.4 of [4]) (10 2 cm/m)𝑙𝑙[m] (𝑙𝑙 = 𝑏𝑏 in eq.4 of [4]) Thickness 𝑡𝑡[m] (10 2 cm/m)𝑡𝑡[m] 10 2 𝑡𝑡 Volume 𝑉𝑉[m 3 ] = 𝑤𝑤𝑙𝑙𝑡𝑡[m 3 ] (10 6 cm 3 /m 3 ) 𝑉𝑉[m 3 ] ΔMagnetization ∆𝑀𝑀[A/m] = Δ𝑚𝑚[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ]/𝑉𝑉[m 3 ] (10 -3 emu/cm 3 • m/A)Δ𝑀𝑀[A/m] (10 -2 m/cm)Δ𝑀𝑀[A/m] 𝑱𝑱 𝒄𝒄,𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎 results 𝟐𝟐𝚫𝚫𝒎𝒎[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ] 𝑽𝑽[m 3 ]𝒘𝒘[m] �𝟏𝟏 - 𝒘𝒘 𝟑𝟑𝒍𝒍 � Result in [𝑨𝑨/𝐦𝐦 𝟐𝟐 ] 𝟐𝟐𝚫𝚫𝒎𝒎[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ] 𝑽𝑽[m 3 ]𝒘𝒘[m] �𝟏𝟏 - 𝒘𝒘 𝟑𝟑𝒍𝒍 � 𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏 -𝟒𝟒 m 2 cm 2 Result in [𝑨𝑨/𝒄𝒄𝒎𝒎 𝟐𝟐 ] 𝟐𝟐𝚫𝚫𝑴𝑴[𝐴𝐴/m] 𝒘𝒘[m] �𝟏𝟏 - 𝒘𝒘 𝟑𝟑𝒍𝒍 � 𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏 -𝟓𝟓 m 2 cm 2 (? ) = 𝟐𝟐𝚫𝚫𝒎𝒎[𝐴𝐴𝑚𝑚 2 ] 𝑽𝑽[m 3 ]𝒘𝒘[m] �𝟏𝟏 - 𝒘𝒘 𝟑𝟑𝒍𝒍 � 𝟏𝟏𝟏𝟏 -𝟓𝟓 m 2 cm 2 (? )

figure.head: Figure 1
figure.figDesc: Figure 1 Schematics for the calculation of 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 in the simplest Bean model scenario. The field is perpendicular to the sample rectangular crosssection 𝑤𝑤 × 𝑙𝑙 = 2𝑎𝑎 × 2𝑏𝑏 (with 𝑎𝑎 < 𝑏𝑏) and the current circulates in rectangular loops with the same absolute value of 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 at all points in the cross-section.
use with cgs-emu input data) (2) with, 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 in A/cm 2 , Δ𝑀𝑀 in [emu/cm 3 ], 𝑤𝑤 and 𝑙𝑙 (being the full cross-section dimensions with 𝑤𝑤 < 𝑙𝑙) in [cm], and 𝑉𝑉 in [cm 3 ] and where 20 Acm 2

figure.head: Formulae
figure.figDesc: resulting in CORRECT 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 values Formulae resulting into INCORRECT 𝑱𝑱 𝒄𝒄,𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎𝒎 values

Since most instruments provide the moment in [emu], using eq. (1) requires first converting such [emu] moments to 𝑚𝑚 [Am 2 ] with the correspondence 1 emu →10 -3 Am 2[9], expressing 𝑤𝑤, 𝑙𝑙 and 𝑡𝑡 in [m] and 𝑉𝑉 in [m 3 ]. If desired, the final conversion from A/m 2 to A/cm 2 is trivial.

note.p: 𝑤𝑤 and 𝑙𝑙 (being the full cross-section dimensions with 𝑤𝑤 < 𝑙𝑙) in [m], and 𝑉𝑉 in [m 3 ].

figure.figDesc: Result in [𝑨𝑨/𝒄𝒄𝒎𝒎 𝟐𝟐 ] dominated the 4.2 K 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values. One of these samples, SP 215, has a remarkably similar BaZrO3 distribution to the "record" sample of ref.[1]. The reported transport 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values of Francis are 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 (20 K, 7 T)∼ 6.2 MA/cm 2 and 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 (4.2 K,8 T)∼ 10.3 MA/cm 2[12], whereas Goyal's original estimations for the BZO sample were 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 (20 K, 7 T)∼ 60 MA/cm 2 and 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐,𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚𝑚 (4.2 K,7 T)∼ 90 MA/cm 2[1]. The huge difference in 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 between Goyal's and Francis' samples should have raised suspicions. Their 𝐽𝐽 𝑐𝑐 values are indeed about a factor of 9-10 apart, due to the error caused by the incorrect conversion.

div.head: Acknowledgement
div.p: This work was performed at the National High Magnetic Field Laboratory, which is supported by National Science Foundation Cooperative Agreements DMR-2128556, and by the State of Florida. Evaluation of REBCO coated conductors is supported by the DOE-Office of Fusion Energy grant DE-SC022011) and by a sub-award from ARPA-E through High Temperature Superconductor Inc.

analytic.title: Significantly enhanced critical current density and pinning force in nanostructured, (RE)BCO-based, coated conductor

monogr.title: Fundamental Nature of the Self-Field Critical Current in Superconductors

analytic.title: Retraction Note: Significantly enhanced critical current density and pinning force in nanostructured, (RE)BCO-based, coated conductor

monogr.title: Expression with self-consistent magnetic units for calculation of critical current density using the Bean's Model

analytic.title: Electromagnetic Units, the Giorgi System, and the Revised International System of Units
Sul sistema di unità di misure elettromagnetiche
Rapid assessment of REBCO CC angular critical current density J c(B, T = 4.2 K, θ) using torque magnetometry up to at least 30 tesla
Angular dependence of Jc for YBCO coated conductors at low temperature and very high magnetic fields
Development of general expressions for the temperature and magnetic field dependence of the critical current density in coated conductors with variable properties
